content.problemStatement:
Once upon a time, Chef had with him an array AAA of length NNN, with elements in [1,N][1, N][1,N].

From this array AAA, he created a new array FFF of length NNN via the following process:


Let PPP be the prefix maximum array of AAA.

That is, PPP is an array of length NNN satisfying Pi=max⁡(A1,A2,…,Ai)P_i = \max(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_i)Pi​=max(A1​,A2​,…,Ai​)
Let SSS be the suffix minimum array of AAA.

That is, SSS is an array of length NNN satisfying Si=min⁡(Ai,Ai+1,…,AN)S_i = \min(A_i, A_{i+1}, \ldots, A_N)Si​=min(Ai​,Ai+1​,…,AN​)
Initialize FFF to all zeros.
For each iii from 111 to NNN, add 111 to both FPiF_{P_i}FPi​​ and FSiF_{S_i}FSi​​.
Essentially, FFF is the frequency array corresponding to the union of arrays PPP and SSS.

For example, if A=[2,3,1,2]A = [2, 3, 1, 2]A=[2,3,1,2], we have P=[2,3,3,3]P = [2, 3, 3, 3]P=[2,3,3,3] and S=[1,1,1,2]S = [1, 1, 1, 2]S=[1,1,1,2], so that F=[3,2,3,0]F = [3, 2, 3, 0]F=[3,2,3,0].

With the passing of time, Chef forgot the array AAA and remembers only the frequency array FFF.

Can you help Chef find any initial array AAA of length NNN that would result in the given array FFF?

Note that the elements of AAA need not be distinct.

**As an additional constraint, in this problem it is guaranteed that the following two conditions will hold:**


F1+F2+…+FN=2NF_1 + F_2 + \ldots + F_N = 2NF1​+F2​+…+FN​=2N
F1≥NF_1 \ge NF1​≥N
It is possible that there might not be any valid initial array AAA that achieves the given frequency array FFF. In this case, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the required array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers F1,F2,…,FNF_1, F_2, \ldots, F_{N}F1​,F2​,…,FN​, the frequency array Chef remembers.




Output Format
For each test case,



If a valid array AAA exists, output NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ on a new line, denoting the array you found.
If no valid array AAA exists, output the single integer −1-1−1 on a new line.

If there are multiple solutions, you may print any of them.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: With A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2], we obtain P=[1,2]P = [1, 2]P=[1,2] and S=[1,2]S = [1, 2]S=[1,2], leading to F=[2,2]F = [2, 2]F=[2,2] as required.
Test case 222: It can be shown that no valid array AAA of length 222 results in F=[3,1]F = [3, 1]F=[3,1].
The possible choices of AAA are [1,1],[1,2],[2,1],[2,2][1, 1], [1, 2], [2, 1], [2, 2][1,1],[1,2],[2,1],[2,2] which result in the FFF arrays [4,0],[2,2],[2,2],[0,4][4, 0], [2, 2], [2, 2], [0, 4][4,0],[2,2],[2,2],[0,4], respectively.
Test case 333: With A=[3,2,1]A = [3, 2, 1]A=[3,2,1] we obtain P=[3,3,3]P = [3, 3, 3]P=[3,3,3] and S=[1,1,1]S = [1, 1, 1]S=[1,1,1], so that F=[3,0,3]F = [3, 0, 3]F=[3,0,3] as required.
